title: Merck — Transplant — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: PREVYMIS (letermovir) in Transplant CMV

Product: PREVYMIS (letermovir) Manufacturer: Merck & Co., Inc., Rahway, N.J., USA Indication: Prophylaxis of cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection and disease in transplant recipients (HSCT and kidney transplant) Document scope: Product-level market context, competitive landscape, commercial dynamics, and indication expansion strategy Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape & Product Overview

1.1 CMV in Transplant Recipients

Cytomegalovirus (CMV) is the most common viral infection following solid organ and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. CMV reactivation or primary infection can cause graft failure, end-organ disease (pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, hepatitis), and death. The clinical burden is defined by donor/recipient serostatus:

  • D+/R- (donor seropositive, recipient seronegative): Highest risk. These patients lack prior CMV immunity and receive a CMV-positive graft. CMV disease rates without prophylaxis can exceed 40-60%
  • R+ (recipient seropositive): Moderate risk. Viral reactivation occurs in 30-80% depending on immunosuppression intensity
  • D-/R- (both negative): Lowest risk. Primary infection via blood products or community exposure

Key epidemiology:

  • CMV viremia occurs in 16-56% of transplant patients (median ~30%)
  • CMV disease develops in 8-32% of kidney transplant recipients depending on serostatus
  • ~30% of allogeneic HSCT recipients develop CMV disease
  • CMV is associated with increased all-cause mortality, graft loss, opportunistic co-infections, and higher healthcare costs

1.2 Transplant Market Sizing

Transplant Type US Annual Volume (2024) CMV High-Risk (D+/R-) Prevymis-Eligible
Kidney transplant ~27,800 ~20-25% (~5,500-6,900) All D+/R- adults + pediatric >=12y
Allogeneic HSCT ~10,400 (allo) ~60-80% seropositive All CMV-seropositive adults + pediatric >=6m
Total HSCT ~22,600 (all) — Allogeneic only

The kidney transplant expansion is transformative. The KT market is approximately 2.7x the size of the allogeneic HSCT market in terms of annual procedures, and the D+/R- population in KT (~5,500-6,900) is comparable to the seropositive HSCT population — effectively doubling Prevymis's addressable patient base.

1.3 PREVYMIS Overview

PREVYMIS (letermovir) is a first-in-class CMV DNA terminase complex inhibitor. Unlike older antivirals (ganciclovir/valganciclovir) that target the viral DNA polymerase, letermovir blocks a distinct step in viral DNA processing — the cleavage of concatemeric CMV DNA into unit-length genomes for packaging into viral capsids.

Key product attributes:

  • Mechanism: CMV DNA terminase complex inhibitor (pUL56 subunit) — novel MOA, no cross-resistance with DNA polymerase inhibitors
  • Formulations: Oral tablets (240 mg, 480 mg), IV injection (20 mg/mL), oral pellets (20 mg, 120 mg/packet)
  • Dosing: 480 mg once daily (reduced to 240 mg with cyclosporine co-administration)
  • No myelosuppression: Unlike valganciclovir, Prevymis does not cause bone marrow toxicity — this is the key clinical differentiator
  • No renal dose adjustment: For CrCl >10 mL/min

1.4 Regulatory Timeline

Date Milestone
Nov 2017 FDA approval for CMV prophylaxis in CMV-seropositive adult allogeneic HSCT recipients
Jun 2023 FDA approval for CMV prophylaxis in high-risk (D+/R-) adult kidney transplant recipients
2024 Pediatric expansion: HSCT recipients >=6 months/>=6 kg; KT recipients >=12 years/>=40 kg
2024 Extended 200-day dosing in high-risk HSCT recipients (previously Day 100)
2024 EC approval for KT and extended HSCT dosing in Europe

1.5 Revenue Trajectory

Period Revenue YoY Growth Driver
FY 2023 ~$625M (est.) — HSCT base + early KT launch
H1 2025 $436M +20.4% KT uptake accelerating
FY 2025 $978M +25% KT launch ramp + international expansion
Q4 2025 $275M +28% Strong US demand + new indications

Prevymis is approaching $1B in annual revenue, driven primarily by the kidney transplant indication expansion. Merck's earnings commentary attributes growth to "higher demand in the U.S. as well as in most international markets, reflecting in part the launch of new indications."

2. Competitive Landscape

2.1 CMV Prophylaxis Competitors

Product Company Mechanism Indication Price (WAC) Key Limitation
Prevymis (letermovir) Merck Terminase inhibitor Prophylaxis (HSCT, KT) $225/day ($6,750/month) Cost; limited to D+/R- in KT
Valganciclovir (generic) Multiple DNA polymerase inhibitor Prophylaxis + treatment ~$8-15/day (generic) Myelosuppression (leukopenia/neutropenia in 64%)
Livtencity (maribavir) Takeda UL97 kinase inhibitor Treatment of refractory CMV ~$300/day Not prophylaxis; refractory/resistant only
CytoGam (CMV-IGIV) CSL Behring Passive immunization Adjunctive prophylaxis Variable (IV) Modest efficacy; used adjunctively

2.2 The Valganciclovir Barrier

The central competitive dynamic in this market is the price-safety tradeoff between Prevymis and generic valganciclovir:

Valganciclovir strengths:

  • Decades of clinical experience and guidelines support
  • Generic pricing (~$8-15/day vs. ~$225/day for Prevymis — a 15-30x cost gap)
  • Effective prophylaxis with well-understood efficacy
  • Familiar to all transplant centers

Valganciclovir weaknesses (Prevymis's opportunity):

  • Bone marrow toxicity: 64% leukopenia/neutropenia rate vs. 26% with Prevymis (P<0.001)
  • Drug discontinuation: 13.5% discontinue due to AEs vs. 4.1% with Prevymis
  • CMV resistance: 12.1% resistance-associated substitutions in trial vs. 0% with Prevymis
  • Patient burden: Frequent CBC monitoring required; dose adjustments for renal function

The persuasion challenge: Prevymis is clinically better tolerated but must justify a dramatic cost premium. The message "no CMV cases with Prevymis vs. 5 cases with valganciclovir through week 28" is the single most effective KT message per ZoomRx message recall data — but it is also the least recalled, indicating an under-delivery opportunity.

2.3 Livtencity (maribavir) — Different Niche

Livtencity (maribavir, Takeda) is not a direct competitor for prophylaxis. It is approved for treatment of CMV infections post-transplant that are refractory to prior therapies (ganciclovir, valganciclovir, cidofovir, foscarnet). It fills a salvage/rescue role:

  • Approved in >30 countries as of 2024
  • Revenue ~$50M (2023 US); projected to reach $600M globally by 2030
  • May expand in non-refractory treatment settings

However, Livtencity does compete for promotional share of voice in the transplant infectious disease space. In ZoomRx message recall data, Livtencity and Prevymis have similar rep reach among HCPs, though Prevymis leads in share of voice.

3. Clinical Evidence Base

3.1 HSCT (Original Indication)

Pivotal trial (KEYNOTE study, 2017):

  • Letermovir vs. placebo in 565 CMV-seropositive adult allogeneic HSCT recipients
  • Primary endpoint: Clinically significant CMV infection through Week 24 — letermovir 37.5% vs. placebo 60.6%
  • Survival benefit: Mortality through Week 48 was lower with letermovir (20.3% vs. 25.5%) — a key promotional message

Extended 200-day prophylaxis:

  • Phase 3 trial assessed Day 200 extended prophylaxis in high-risk adults (vs. standard Day 100)
  • Significant efficacy demonstrated from ~Day 100 to ~Day 200
  • Now part of the label and ASTCT guidelines recommend Prevymis for CMV-seropositive HSCT recipients

3.2 Kidney Transplant (Expansion Indication)

Pivotal Phase 3 trial (NCT03443869):

  • 589 adult D+/R- kidney transplant recipients, randomized to Prevymis + acyclovir vs. valganciclovir + placebo
  • Primary endpoint: CMV disease through Week 52 — Prevymis 10.4% vs. valganciclovir 11.8% (noninferiority met)
  • CMV DNAemia by Week 28: 2.1% vs. 8.8% — Prevymis substantially better
  • Leukopenia/neutropenia through Week 28: 26% vs. 64% (difference: -37.9 percentage points, P<0.001)
  • Discontinuation due to AEs: 4.1% vs. 13.5%
  • Resistance: 0% with Prevymis vs. 12.1% with valganciclovir
  • Most common AE: Diarrhea (32% vs. 29%)

The KT trial tells a clear story: Prevymis is noninferior on CMV disease prevention and dramatically superior on tolerability. The bone marrow toxicity difference (26% vs. 64%) is the anchor message.

4. ZoomRx Research Portfolio

4.1 Active Studies

Study Type Fielding Sample Key Focus
Message Recall Study (KT + HSCT) PET/Message Recall May-Aug 2025 (Wave 1) KT: 102 HCPs, HSCT: 93 HCPs Share of voice, message recall & effectiveness, rep performance
KT PET Full Report Promotional Effectiveness Tracking May-Aug 2025 102 KT HCPs, 93 HSCT HCPs Same study, alternate v1 report
KT ATU (Wave 2) Awareness, Trial & Usage 2024 (Wave 2) Kidney transplant treaters Awareness, usage, perceptions, drivers/barriers

4.2 Key Findings from ZoomRx Studies

Share of Voice (SOV):

  • Prevymis leads market in both rep interactions and NPP, outpacing Livtencity and CytoGam

  • 90% of HCPs exposed to Prevymis non-personal promotions (>40% SOV)

  • Among ID physicians and pharmacists at dual KT/HSCT institutions: 75% receive both KT + HSCT discussions, with KT prioritized (discussed first, more time spent)

KT Message Performance:

  • Highest recall: KT9 (lower leukopenia/neutropenia: 26% vs. 64%) and KT1 (adult indication)
  • Most effective but under-recalled: KT7 ("No CMV cases with Prevymis vs. 5 cases with valganciclovir through Week 28") — high perceived effectiveness but lowest recall. This represents the single biggest messaging opportunity
  • Message effectiveness: Overall KT messages exceed industry averages (68% top message vs. 64% IA)
  • Community HCPs rated messages more effectively; academic HCPs showed higher prescribing intent

HSCT Message Performance:

  • Highest recall: HSCT10 (200-day trial design) and HSCT1 (indication)
  • Most effective but under-recalled: HSCT4 (survival benefit through Week 48) and HSCT5 (200-day efficacy) — perceived as highly effective but trail industry averages in recall
  • Key gap: HSCT efficacy messages (the most differentiating) are not being delivered frequently enough

Rep Performance:

  • 75% of KT and HSCT interactions rated high quality (6,7 on 7-point scale)

  • 50% of HCPs anticipate increasing Prevymis prescribing post-interaction (vs. 49% industry average)

  • 88% of KT interactions drove at least one action (reviewing data, enrolling in PSP)
  • KT rep performance drivers: Knowledge of competitor products, providing a compelling reason to prescribe, asking questions effectively
  • HSCT rep performance drivers: Knowledge of insurance/PSPs, providing a compelling reason to prescribe, providing credible support

Access Concerns:

  • HCPs want to hear more about insurance/reimbursement and Prevymis coupon — cost remains a real barrier
  • Verbatim: "One of the major hurdles to obtaining letermovir for my patients is the cost and it is a major headache to obtain it for all my patients who need it"
  • Merck Access Program (MAP) supports insurance, prior auths, co-pay help, and referrals
  • Privately insured patients may pay as little as $15/Rx (up to $2,500 savings per Rx)

5. Market Dynamics & Strategic Implications

5.1 The KT Expansion Is the Growth Story

Prevymis's trajectory from ~$625M (2023) to $978M (2025) is driven primarily by kidney transplant uptake. The math:

  • ~27,800 kidney transplants/year in the US (2024, record high)
  • ~20-25% are D+/R- (~5,500-6,900 eligible patients)
  • At $225/day x 200 days = ~$45,000 per patient per prophylaxis course
  • Peak penetration of D+/R- at 50% = ~$125-155M incremental annual US revenue
  • Plus R+ patients if indication expands = potentially 2-3x larger

The international launch (EC approval 2024) multiplies this further.

5.2 The Price Barrier Is Real but Manageable

The 15-30x cost gap vs. generic valganciclovir is Prevymis's biggest headwind. But several factors work in its favor:

  1. Myelosuppression avoidance: The 64% vs. 26% leukopenia rate is clinically meaningful — patients on valganciclovir require frequent CBCs and dose adjustments, adding monitoring costs
  2. Discontinuation rates: 13.5% vs. 4.1% AE-related discontinuation means fewer treatment failures and switches
  3. Zero resistance: 0% vs. 12.1% resistance substitutions — resistance management is expensive and dangerous
  4. Institutional formulary decisions: Hospital P&T committees, not individual physicians, often control access — total cost-of-care arguments (fewer labs, fewer switches, fewer resistance cases) can win formulary placement

5.3 Indication Expansion Beyond D+/R-

The current KT approval is limited to D+/R- (highest risk) patients. The broader KT market includes:

  • R+ patients: Moderate risk, much larger population (~50-60% of all KT recipients)
  • Other solid organ transplants: Liver, heart, lung — each with significant CMV burden
  • If Merck pursues R+ or broader SOT indications, the addressable market grows dramatically

5.4 Competitive Moat

Prevymis has a strong competitive position:

  • First-in-class mechanism: No other terminase inhibitors approved or in late-stage development
  • No cross-resistance: Uniquely positioned for resistance-prone patients
  • Safety profile: Fundamentally different from DNA polymerase inhibitors — no myelosuppression
  • Dual indication: Only product approved for both HSCT and KT CMV prophylaxis
  • Pediatric expansion: Extended the label to both HSCT (>=6 months) and KT (>=12 years)

6. Key Contacts & Stakeholder Map

6.1 ZoomRx Team (from ATU Proposal)

  • Senior Leadership: Dave Johnson (Principal), Melissa Weinstein (Senior Manager)
  • Execution: Alex Colborn (Engagement Manager)
  • ZoomRx has partnered with Merck franchises since 2015; active on Prevymis since at least 2019

6.2 Target HCP Segments

  • Transplant nephrology: Primary prescribers for KT; rate Prevymis reps highly on product knowledge
  • Infectious disease: Manage CMV across both KT and HSCT; higher message recall but lower prescribing intent vs. nephrologists
  • Hematology-oncology: Primary prescribers for HSCT; stronger prescribing intent than ID physicians
  • Transplant pharmacists: Key influencers on formulary decisions; want more safety profile information
  • NP/PAs: Higher call quality ratings and prescribing intent than physicians; perceived messages more favorably

The KT trial tells a clear story: Prevymis is noninferior on CMV disease prevention and dramatically superior on tolerability. The bone marrow toxicity difference (26% vs. 64%) is the anchor message.

R+ patients: Moderate risk, much larger population (~50-60% of all KT recipients)
Other solid organ transplants: Liver, heart, lung — each with significant CMV burden
If Merck pursues R+ or broader SOT indications, the addressable market grows dramatically
